criteria: The agent has read Section 2.1 completely to understand the auto-regressive language model notation, vocabulary V, and the factorized joint probability formulation in Equation 1.
The agent has read Section 2.2 completely to understand the formal definition of Extractable Memorization (Definition 1), including the greedy decoding condition for verbatim reproduction.
The agent has read Section 3 completely to understand the TOKENSWAP algorithm, including how the main and auxiliary model probabilities are combined using a scaling factor α.

The agent has read Algorithm 1 carefully to understand the per-token procedure: querying both models, computing the normalization factor α, applying selective probability replacement for tokens in G, and sampling the next token.

The agent has read Section 3 to understand the criteria for selecting G: tokens must be high-frequency, grammar-based (e.g., function words), and their replacement must not degrade task-specific capabilities.

The agent has read Appendix C.2 completely to understand how G is constructed: starting from the 500 most frequent COCA words, applying NLTK POS filtering to retain determiners, prepositions, conjunctions, pronouns, modal verbs, wh-words, and auxiliary verbs, yielding |G| = 110 tokens.

The agent has read Section 4.1 completely to understand the extreme memorization experimental setup: fine-tuning Llama-3.2-3B on 2,000 sequences from MathAbstracts and WritingPrompts for 50 epochs, using a 20-token prefix and 128-token greedy sampling.

The agent has read Section 4.2 completely to understand the real-world memorization evaluation setup on Pile-Memorized (Pythia-6.9B, 184 examples, 32-token prefix, 48-token suffix) and LeetCode (Llama-3-8B, 1,825 problems, 20-token prefix, 100 tokens sampled).
The agent has read Section 4.3 to understand the comparison with Goldfish: using pre-trained Goldfish models with k∈{3,4,32} trained on Wikipedia sequences duplicated 50 times in RedPajama.

The agent has read Table 2 to check the expected extreme memorization results: ML, EMR, ROUGE-L, and Levenshtein scores for Standard, MemFree, CP-Fuse-mix, CP-Fuse-half, and TOKENSWAP on WritingPrompts and MathAbstracts.

The agent has read Table 3 to check the expected real-world memorization results on LeetCode (Llama-3-8B) and Pile-Memorized (Pythia-6.9B), including performance metrics CE Loss, MT-Bench, and commonsense reasoning accuracy.
The agent has read Appendix C.1.2 to check the fine-tuning hyperparameters: sequence length 2048, batch size 1, learning rate 5×10^-5, AdamW optimizer, gradient accumulation steps 1, warmup steps 50.
The plan includes an implementation of the TOKENSWAP algorithm (Algorithm 1) using PyTorch and HuggingFace, with a main model and a small auxiliary model running in parallel at inference time.
The plan includes construction of the token subset G using the 500 most frequent COCA words filtered by NLTK POS tags to yield 110 grammar-based tokens.
The plan includes fine-tuning Llama-3.2-3B on 2,000 randomly sampled sequences each from MathAbstracts and WritingPrompts datasets for 50 epochs with the specified hyperparameters.
The plan includes loading and preprocessing the Pile-Memorized dataset (184 filtered natural language sequences with 32-token prefixes and 48-token suffixes) for evaluation on Pythia-6.9B.
The plan includes loading and preprocessing the LeetCode dataset (1,825 problems with punctuation removed, using 20-token prefixes and 100-token greedy sampling) for evaluation on Llama-3-8B.
The plan includes implementing all memorization metrics: Matching Length (ML), Exact Match Rate (EMR), ROUGE-L, and Normalized Levenshtein Distance.

The plan includes implementing inference-time baselines: Standard (greedy decoding), MemFree (blocking n-gram matches), CP-Fuse (logit combination from disjoint models), for the extreme memorization experiment.

The plan includes implementing performance metrics: Cross-Entropy Loss on SlimPajama, MT-Bench evaluation (with GPT-4 as judge), and 5-shot commonsense reasoning benchmarks (BoolQ, SIQA, PIQA, ARC-Challenge, ARC-Easy, OBQA, WinoGrande).
The plan includes an ablation study on the size of G, constructing it from the top-k most frequent words for k∈{10, 50, 100, 500, 2500} and evaluating on the Pile-memorized task.
The plan includes an ablation study on auxiliary model size using SmolLM variants (135M, 360M, 1.7B) evaluated on both LeetCode (Llama-3-8B) and Pile-Memorized (Pythia-6.9B) datasets.
The plan includes evaluation of TOKENSWAP across the Pythia model family (70M to 6.9B) using Pythia-70M as the auxiliary model to demonstrate performance-memorization tradeoff.

The core TOKENSWAP algorithm (Algorithm 1) has been implemented: at each decoding step, both the main and auxiliary model distributions are queried, the scaling factor α is computed as the ratio of the total probability mass assigned to G by the main model to that assigned by the auxiliary model, and token probabilities are selectively replaced.

The scaling factor α computation has been implemented correctly as α = sum(p_i^main[v] for v in G) / sum(p_i^aux[v] for v in G), ensuring the final distribution p_i^final remains a valid probability distribution.
The selective token replacement has been implemented: for tokens v NOT in G, p_i^final[v] = p_i^main[v]; for tokens v IN G, p_i^final[v] = α * p_i^aux[v].
The next token is sampled from p_i^final (line 8 of Algorithm 1), and the generated token is appended to the context for subsequent steps.

The token subset G has been constructed by starting with the 500 most frequent English words from COCA, applying NLTK POS tagging to retain only grammatical function words (DT, IN, CC, PRP, PRP$, MD, WDT, WP, WRB) and auxiliary verbs (be, do, have), yielding |G| = 110 tokens.

Tokenizer consistency has been verified: all 110 grammar-based tokens in G appear as single tokens across the vocabularies of all models used (GPT-2/Pythia BPE, LLaMA-3 WordPiece, OLMo-2 Unigram, SmolLM Byte-BPE), enabling cross-tokenizer application without vocabulary alignment.

The fine-tuning of Llama-3.2-3B has been implemented with 2,000 randomly sampled sequences from both MathAbstracts (AutoMathText) and WritingPrompts datasets, training for 50 epochs with hyperparameters: sequence length 2048, batch size 1, learning rate 5×10^-5, AdamW optimizer, gradient accumulation steps 1, warmup steps 50.
The MathAbstracts and WritingPrompts datasets have been downloaded from HuggingFace, with 2,000 training examples sampled using a fixed seed and 500 distinct evaluation points sampled separately.

The Pile-Memorized dataset has been loaded: 184 examples of memorized sequences (filtered to natural language only) from the Pile dataset with 32-token prefixes and 48-token suffixes as identified by Chang et al.
The LeetCode dataset has been loaded: 1,825 problem statements downloaded from Kaggle, with punctuation removed for evaluation, and using a 20-token prefix to generate the next 100 tokens.
The Matching Length (ML) metric has been implemented: it measures the number of verbatim characters or tokens generated before the first deviation from the reference.
The Exact Match Rate (EMR) metric has been implemented: it computes the fraction of sequences reproduced verbatim via greedy decoding.
The ROUGE-L metric has been implemented as ROUGE-L = LCS / len(reference text), where LCS is the longest common subsequence length between generated and reference text (Equation 2).

The Normalized Levenshtein Distance metric has been implemented: it quantifies the minimum number of character-level edits needed to transform generated text into the original, normalized to [0,1], where larger values indicate lower memorization.
The Cross-Entropy Loss performance metric (Equation 3) has been implemented to evaluate on SlimPajama dataset samples: CE = -sum_i P(x_i) log Q(x_i).
The MemFree baseline has been implemented: it blocks next-token outputs that would create exact n-gram matches with the training data.

The CP-Fuse baseline has been implemented in two variants for the extreme memorization experiment: CP-FUSE-HALF (disjoint 1,000-sequence splits) and CP-FUSE-MIXTURE (1,500-sequence splits with 500 overlapping), using the public CP-Fuse implementation.
The CP-Fuse baseline for real-world evaluation (Section 4.2) has been implemented using model pairs: Pythia-2.8B paired with Pythia-6.9B, and Llama-3.2-3B paired with Llama-3-8B.

The commonsense reasoning evaluation has been implemented: 5-shot evaluation across 128-sample subsets of WinoGrande, PIQA, OpenBookQA, BoolQ, ARC-Easy, and ARC-Challenge, generating the next 10 tokens per example.
The MT-Bench evaluation has been implemented using GPT-4 as a judge to assess multi-turn conversation, instruction-following, and generation quality.

The TOKENSWAP inference using DistilGPT-2 (80M) as the auxiliary model has been implemented for the extreme memorization experiments (Section 4.1), with G = 110 tokens and 20-token prefix, 128-token greedy sampling.
The TOKENSWAP inference using SmolLM-135M as the auxiliary model has been implemented for the LeetCode evaluation on Llama-3-8B (an instruction-tuned auxiliary is needed for MT-Bench evaluation).

The Goldfish comparison experiment has been implemented by loading pre-trained Goldfish models (k∈{3,4,32}) from the public repository and evaluating on the Wikipedia sequences duplicated 50 times in RedPajama.
The ROUGE-L > 0.8 threshold has been implemented as the memorization criterion for the LeetCode dataset (instead of exact match rate), with appropriate preprocessing (punctuation removal).
The Fractional Exact Rate (FER) metric has been implemented: it computes the fraction of tokens identical at the same position between generated and reference text.

The OLMo-2-13B evaluation has been implemented: 5,000 random Wikipedia sequences from Dolma corpus, prompted with 50-token prefixes to generate 50 tokens, with ROUGE-L > 0.9 used to identify memorized sequences, and OLMo-2-7B used for CP-Fuse.

The ablation on G size has been implemented: constructing G from the top-k most frequent English words for k∈{10, 50, 100, 500, 2500}, yielding |G|∈{9, 43, 66, 110, 136} tokens, and evaluating on Pile-memorized with Pythia-6.9B and Pythia-70M.
The ablation on auxiliary model size has been implemented using the SmolLM family (135M, 360M, 1.7B) on both LeetCode (Llama-3-8B) and Pile-Memorized (Pythia-6.9B).

The performance-memorization tradeoff analysis has been implemented across seven Pythia model sizes (70M to 6.9B) using Pythia-70M as the auxiliary model, computing EMR vs. CE Loss for both Standard and TOKENSWAP.

The speculative decoding comparison has been implemented: using Pythia-6.9B as main model and Pythia-70M as auxiliary with γ=5 proposed tokens and temperature T=1.0, evaluated on the Pile-Memorized dataset.

The extreme memorization experiment (Section 4.1) has been executed successfully without errors: fine-tuning Llama-3.2-3B and evaluating Standard, MemFree, CP-Fuse-half, CP-Fuse-mix, and TOKENSWAP on both WritingPrompts and MathAbstracts with all four memorization metrics.
The real-world memorization evaluation (Section 4.2) on the Pile-Memorized dataset has been executed successfully without errors on Pythia-6.9B, comparing Standard, CP-Fuse, and TOKENSWAP.

The real-world memorization evaluation (Section 4.2) on the LeetCode dataset has been executed successfully without errors on Llama-3-8B, comparing Standard, CP-Fuse, TOKENSWAP with DistilGPT-2, and TOKENSWAP with SmolLM-135M, including MT-Bench and commonsense reasoning benchmarks.

The Goldfish comparison experiment (Section 4.3) has been executed successfully without errors, evaluating TOKENSWAP against Standard and Goldfish (k=3,4,32) on all five metrics (ML, EMR, ROUGE-L, Levenshtein, CE Loss).
The OLMo-2-13B evaluation experiment (Appendix B.1) has been executed successfully without errors.
The G-size ablation experiment (Appendix B.5) has been executed successfully without errors across all five values of k.
The auxiliary model size ablation experiment (Appendix B.6) has been executed successfully without errors for all SmolLM variants on both LeetCode and Pile-Memorized datasets.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2 for WritingPrompts: TOKENSWAP reduces EMR from 83.4% to approximately 0.1% (~800× reduction) and ROUGE-L from 0.89 to 0.19 (~4.6× reduction) compared to Standard generation.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2 for MathAbstracts: TOKENSWAP reduces EMR from 93.6% to approximately 1.8% (~50× reduction) and ROUGE-L from 0.98 to 0.38 (~2.6× reduction) compared to Standard generation.

The execution outcomes match Table 2: MemFree achieves EMR=0 but with higher ROUGE-L and lower Levenshtein than TOKENSWAP, demonstrating it prevents exact verbatim generation but allows near-verbatim generation.

The execution outcomes match Table 3 for the Pile-Memorized (Pythia-6.9B): TOKENSWAP reduces EMR from 65.22% (Standard) to approximately 5.98% (~10× reduction) and ML from 151.6 to approximately 35.10.

The execution outcomes match Table 3 for the LeetCode (Llama-3-8B): TOKENSWAP with DistilGPT-2 reduces ROUGE-L>0.8 from 9.65% to approximately 0.96%, while maintaining commonsense reasoning accuracy at 71.87% (same as Standard).

The execution outcomes match Table 3: TOKENSWAP with SmolLM-135M achieves MT-Bench score approximately 7.78, comparable to Standard (7.75), demonstrating preservation of instruction-following and conversational abilities.

The execution outcomes match Table 4: TOKENSWAP achieves comparable or better ML (~12.4) and ROUGE-L (~0.22) and Levenshtein (~0.72) to Goldfish (k=3) while maintaining a lower CE Loss (~3.44 vs. 3.54), demonstrating that TOKENSWAP matches aggressive pre-training methods without modifying training.

The execution outcomes match Table 4: combining TOKENSWAP with Goldfish (k=3) as the main model achieves lower ML (~7.9) than either method alone, demonstrating the two approaches are orthogonal and complementary.

The execution outcomes match Table 5 (OLMo-2-13B): TOKENSWAP completely eliminates exact verbatim generation (EMR=0) and substantially reduces ROUGE-L from 0.95 to approximately 0.38 and Levenshtein from 0.07 to approximately 0.60.

The execution outcomes match Table 9 (G-size ablation): as |G| increases from 9 to 136, EMR decreases monotonically from ~22.28% to ~8.15%, while CE Loss remains stable (~2.87), confirming that larger G enables more memorization disruption with negligible performance cost.

The execution outcomes match Table 10 (auxiliary model ablation): smaller auxiliary models lead to greater memorization reduction (e.g., DistilGPT-2 achieves lower EMR than SmolLM-1.7B), while performance metrics (CE Loss) show minimal variation across auxiliary model sizes.

The execution outcomes match Appendix B.8 (Table 12): across the Pythia model family, TOKENSWAP significantly reduces EMR and ROUGE-L at each model size while incurring only a ~0.1 increase in CE Loss, confirming a substantially improved performance-memorization tradeoff compared to standard models.

The execution outcomes match Table 13 (speculative decoding comparison): speculative decoding with γ=5, T=1.0 yields ~4× higher EMR than TOKENSWAP with greedy decoding, confirming that speculative decoding fails to mitigate verbatim generation.

The execution outcomes match Table 7 (commonsense reasoning): TOKENSWAP achieves identical accuracy to Standard across all six benchmarks (WinoGrande, PIQA, OpenBookQA, BoolQ, ARC-E, ARC-C), confirming no degradation in reasoning ability.

The execution outcomes match Table 8 (FER results): TOKENSWAP achieves the lowest Fractional Exact Rate across all three real-world datasets (LeetCode: 0.11, Pile-Memorized: 0.26, Wikipedia: 0.17) compared to Standard and CP-Fuse.

The execution outcomes match Table 6 (cross-entropy for extreme memorization): TOKENSWAP achieves lower CE Loss than Standard on both WritingPrompts (5.98 vs. 6.68) and MathAbstracts (4.65 vs. 4.94), while CP-Fuse variants incur substantially higher CE Loss.
